race.name: La Marmotte
race.tagline: Four Tour de France cols. 17,000 feet of climbing. Finish atop Alpe d'Huez. The original French cyclosportive.

vitals.registration: Online via marmottegranfondoalpes.com. Tiered pricing opens October/November. Sells out within hours. Cost: EUR110-130
vitals.feed_zones: 4 official feed stations: Col du Glandon, Valloire, Col du Galibier (tunnel level), Bourg d'Oisans (before Alpe d'Huez).
vitals.cutoff_time: Must pass base of Alpe d'Huez by 18:15 (6:15 PM). Strict enforcement.
route_options: La Marmotte: 177 km (110 mi), 5,180 m gain — the full event
La Rando des Marmottes: 2-day version (97 km + 80 km) — same cols over two days

climate.description: A single day spanning near-freezing summit conditions at 2,642m and potential 35C (95F) heat on Alpe d'Huez. The Galibier summit can see snow, ice, and violent winds even in late June. Valley starts are cool (15-20C) but warm rapidly.
challenges: Galibier summit: 5-10C with wind chill well below zero
Alpe d'Huez in afternoon heat: potentially 30-35C (86-95F)
Must dress for freezing and scorching in the same ride
Sudden thunderstorms build from noon onward — lightning at altitude is real

terrain.surface: Well-maintained French departmental roads (D-roads). All tarmac throughout. No gravel or unpaved sections.
features: Col du Glandon (1,924m) — 21 km opener, relentless grinder
Col du Telegraphe (1,566m) — 11.9 km at 7.1%, warm-up for what follows
Col du Galibier (2,642m) — the crux, with ramps of 10-15% near the summit
Alpe d'Huez (1,860m) — 13.8 km at 8.1%, 21 legendary hairpins on depleted legs

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 87. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 86.

course_description.character: Pure Alpine suffering. Almost exclusively climbing or descending — there is virtually no flat on this route. The four cols are stacked in ascending psychological impact: the Glandon is honest, the Telegraphe is a warm-up, the Galibier is the crux, and Alpe d'Huez is the cherry on top of 163 km of pain. By the time you reach the 21 hairpins, you've already done more climbing than most sportives contain in total.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Galibier at 2,642m is the crux — cycling's most famous high pass, with altitude effects, weather exposure, and brutal gradients. But the real signature is the entire package: four legendary cols stacked in a single day, finishing on Alpe d'Huez's 21 hairpins.

logistics.airport: Geneva (GVA) — 2 hours (best option, most flights). Lyon (LYS) — 2.5 hours. Grenoble (GNB) — 1 hour (limited flights).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Alpe d'Huez (finish) and shuttle/drive to Bourg d'Oisans (start) on race morning. The resort has hotels, apartments, and chalets but books out 6-12 months in advance for event week. Alternatively stay in Bourg d'Oisans for easy start access.
logistics.food: French Alpine cuisine — fondue, tartiflette, hearty mountain food. Carb loading is easy. Feed stations provide bananas, gels, cakes, cola, water.

history.origin_story: France's first cyclosportive, created in 1982 to allow amateur riders to experience the great Tour de France passes of the Oisans massif. Often called 'La Doyenne' (the old lady — shared with Liege-Bastogne-Liege). Predates the Etape du Tour by a decade. The fixed route — Glandon, Telegraphe, Galibier, Alpe d'Huez — has remained largely unchanged for 40+ years, making it cycling's most permanent benchmark.
notable_moments: Route unchanged for 40+ years — the permanent Alpine benchmark
Gold/Silver/Bronze diploma system becomes one of cycling's most respected amateur achievements
7,000+ riders, selling out within hours each year
history.reputation: The original and the benchmark. What the Boston Marathon is to running, La Marmotte is to amateur road cycling.

biased_opinion.summary: La Marmotte is the original. Since 1982, it has set the standard for what a mountain cyclosportive should be: four genuine Tour de France cols, 5,000+ meters of climbing, and a finish atop cycling's most famous mountain. It isn't the hardest event in the world — but it might be the most perfectly designed one. The Glandon opens your legs, the Telegraphe-Galibier block breaks them, and Alpe d'Huez asks you to find something you didn't know you had. The Gold/Silver/Bronze diploma system gives you a permanent benchmark, and the knowledge that the route never changes means your time means something forever.
strengths: The route: four iconic Tour de France cols in a single day, unchanged for 40+ years
Finishing atop Alpe d'Huez: 21 hairpins lined with fans cheering you to the summit
The Gold/Silver/Bronze system: a permanent, comparable benchmark for your cycling achievement
Alpine grandeur: the Galibier summit at 2,642m is cycling at its most epic
weaknesses: Remote access: 2+ hours from the nearest major airport to a small Alpine town
Weather roulette: near-freezing at Galibier, potentially scorching at Alpe d'Huez, in the same ride
The cutoff: 18:15 at the base of Alpe d'Huez means genuine time pressure all day — no leisure riding
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want to test yourself against the mountains that made cycling legends, La Marmotte is where you do it. The Galibier will humble you, and Alpe d'Huez will reward you.

final_verdict.one_liner: France's original cyclosportive. Four Tour de France cols. 5,000m of climbing. Finish atop Alpe d'Huez.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you can sustain 8-12 hours of Alpine climbing and want the definitive mountain cycling experience. Yes if you want a permanent benchmark against one of cycling's most storied courses. Skip if you need technical terrain — this is pure road climbing. Skip if altitude above 2,600m concerns you.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want similar Alpine challenge with more distance options: try Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo. If you want Italian Dolomites instead of French Alps: try Sportful Dolomiti Race. If you want the Tour de France experience with a changing route: try L'Etape du Tour.

videos.title: Descending from the top of Col Du Galibier to Bourg-d'Oisans - La Marmotte 2022
videos.description: Descending from the top of the Col Du Galibier to the last feed station in Bourg-d'Oisans in La Marmotte 2022.

videos.title: La Marmotte 2021 | Unofficial version
videos.description:
Vier baanbuffels waagden zich aan La Marmotte. Met véél te weinig trainingskilometers maar een berg motivatie zakten zij af naar Bourg d'Oisans.

Of ze het wel degelijk haalden, blijft een raadsel. Kapot waren ze allessinds en die ervaring nemen ze ze niet meer af.

Zet je goed en geniet van een video die je best niet te serieus neemt.

videos.title: How to get your best result at the Marmotte 2023
videos.description:
The two most important things to get right for a successful ride at the famously challenging French Granfondo are PACING and NUTRITION.
Alpine Cols coach Marvin explains how to determine your pace based on evidence (not hope!) and how to fuel properly for a successful ride.
See also the 2022 version of this presentation for additional factors to consider, including 5 mistakes to avoid and 5 go-faster tips. https://youtu.be/23koS9mcQ9w 

Contents of this video:
00:30  The course
01:20  Taper: the last 2 weeks
03:30  Carb loading
05:00  Your objective at the event?
08:02  Pacing is the art of finding your limit
08:40  Key pacing decisions
14:55  Your pace should be based on evidence, not hope
16:30  Several examples
22:55  Adjust up or down on the day based upon...
23:30  Nutrition & hydration
27:30  Summary
28:40  Q&A

videos.curation_reason: Recent expert coaching content with specific race strategy and course analysis from experienced Alpine Cols coach.

videos.title: LEPAPE Marmotte Granfondo Alpes 2021
videos.description:
175km 5000m elevation
Official Time 7 hrs 44 mins 29 seconds (342/1928)
FTP 315 73kg

Didn't start until 7.26am (gps troubles), so that's why it's so quiet at the beginning, caught back up to larger groups on the Glandon. Struggled hard on the Galibier with elevation and nutrition.

00:00:00 - Start
00:29:25 - Col du Glandon
01:55:11 - Col du Glandon descent
02:27:06 - Saint-Jean to Saint-Michael
03:12:22 - Col du Télégraphe
04:03:43 - Col du Télégraphe descent
04:12:20 - Col du Galibier
05:36:43 - Col du Galibier descent
05:49:54 - Alpe d'Huez

The event takes begins in Bourg d'Oisan and takes on the Col du Glandon, Col du Télégraphe, Col du Galibier before arriving back in Bourg d'Oisans and finishing at the summit of Alpe d'Huez.
videos.curation_reason: Complete ride-along footage with detailed performance metrics and timestamps for each major climb.

videos.title: Surviving The Marmotte // One of the HARDEST One Day Cycling Events  // 170km 5000m+
videos.description:
Well done to every soul that set foot on this route and has done in the past, It's one of the hardest days on the bike for me and I'm sure multiple others! Although I wasn't entirely happy with the way this video came out because of my mental and physical state during the ride, and meant I couldn't think about making a video and turning the pedals the same time. Nevertheless I hope it brings you some entertainment :)

Drop a LIKE and enjoy!

Become a channel member or shout me a coffee (or cake) to support further - (https://www.buymeacoffee.com/edlaverack)

Thank you for watching this video. I hope that you keep up with the regular videos I post on the channel, subscribe, and share your learnings with those that need to hear it. Your comments are important to me, so please take a second and say ‘Hey’ ;)
---------------------------------------------------------------
F I N D  M E  ON...
🍌 MY STRAVA [https://www.strava.com/pros/47597](https://www.strava.com/pros/47597)
📸 INSTAGRAM [http://instagram.com/edlaverack](http://instagram.com/edlaverack)
🤖 TWITTER [https://twitter.com/edlaverack](https://twitter.com/edlaverack)

Shout me a coffee (or cake) to support further - (https://www.buymeacoffee.com/edlaverack)
Interested in Coaching/Mentoring? - laverackcoaching@yahoo.com

Special thanks to my sponsors for keeping me on the road -
- Factor Bikes via Vires Velo - [https://viresvelo.com/](https://viresvelo.com/)
- Fuelled by STYRKR! [https://styrkr.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=styrkr&utm_content=edlaverack](https://styrkr.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=styrkr&utm_content=edlaverack) - use EDLAV25 for 25% off your order!
- Clifton Coffee - ED15 for 15% OFF COFFEE on our website. No minimum spend required! [https://cliftoncoffee.co.uk/](https://cliftoncoffee.co.uk/)
- Band of Climbers - Affiliate link (small bonus for me) [http://bit.ly/BoC-ED](http://bit.ly/BoC-ED) and use EDMODE10 for 10% off
- Interested in a Haute Route Event? - [https://info.hauteroute.org/promo-code-laverack](https://info.hauteroute.org/promo-code-laverack)
- Y Beic - Repairs, Servicing, Building & Fitting - “Serving the bike AND the rider” -
[https://www.ybeic.co.uk/](https://www.ybeic.co.uk/)
- KitBrix - EdLaverack10 for 10% discount
- KOM Designs - KOM HuntTV (Scott Huish)
----------------------------------------------------------------
A little about Ed….
Ed is an elite cyclist, 2019 British Hill Climb Champion and former Under 23 British RR Champion. He has raced as a Continental level Professional Rider in Japan, Korea, Australia, Canada, Azerbaijan to name a few. He is also a public speaker, coach, and remains grounded with regular returns to his roots at Bynea Cycling Club. At the start of 2021, the Discord community began, and plonked many of Ed's viewers in to a 550+ strong group of people that had many things in common. Not least wanting to explore the world and pedal their bikes faster!

Ed coaches lots of cyclists in the online community too! If you are interested, email [laverackcoaching@yahoo.com](mailto:laverackcoaching@yahoo.com) for information on the 1-2-1 coaching package and training plans, of which there are many!

Ed now prioritises having fun with my hard earned fitness and knowledge in the sport. Showcasing that maybe there is an alternative to racing for teams when you can race for yourself and your community of followers and brands. This YouTube channel is a docu-series highlighting what it’s like to be a cyclist in today’s competitive age of sport.
---------------------------------------------------------
GUYS! DISCLAIMER🙏🏼: Links included in this description might be affiliate links. If you purchase a product or service with the links that I provide I may receive a small commission. There is no additional charge to you. Thank you for supporting my channel so I can continue to provide you with content each week.🤳🏻

videos.curation_reason: Popular first-person race experience with detailed preparation insights and honest perspective on the challenge.

quotes.text: It's one of the hardest grand fondles in the world - one of the hardest days on the bike for me and I'm sure multiple others! I'm just hoping it's not going to be cooking tomorrow because that's when I do tend to suffer.

The Marmot is a major physical and mental challenge including four huge climbs which add up in total to over 65 kilometers of climbing, 5000 meters vertical - it's a day that's significantly longer than a typical Tour de France day in the mountains. Most people will take between 8 and 12 hours to finish and a large number of people will abandon.

key_challenges.name: Four huge climbs
key_challenges.description: 65 kilometers of climbing total with 5,000 meters vertical - significantly longer than a typical Tour de France mountain day. Most people take 8-12 hours to finish and a large number abandon.

terrain_notes.text: The balcony road from bend six on Alpe d'Huez is described as one of the more scenic roads but also one of the more scary, with dramatic elevation changes of about 800 meters.

gear_mentions.text: Running 28mm Continental Grand Prix 5000 tires at around 70 psi, though the rider mentions not being super precise about pressure and adjusting by feel.
6.6 kilogram bike setup with Shimano Dura-Ace Di2 electronic shifting, disc brakes, 500ml bottles with 60 grams carbohydrate mix per bottle using StokeĀ fuel.

race_day_tips.text: Two essential things to get right for a successful ride: pacing especially on the climbs, and fueling. These are critical for the 8-12 hour event.

Carb loading strategy: eat an extra serving of carbs per meal on Thursday and Friday before the race (D minus 3 and D minus 2). Compensate by eating less fat those two days, then eat normally on Saturday.
Reduce training volume by at least 50 percent in the final few days and limit intensity. Taper should be longer if you're older, less fit, or recover slowly.

additional_quotes.text: Continue to train normally until about five or ten days before the event. If you're in any doubt it's better to back off sooner and be conservative - training hard right up to the event is very risky for little payoff.
The goal of the taper is to eliminate fatigue without losing any fitness. My general suggestion is to reduce your training volume by at least 50 percent in the final few days.

rider_intel.search_text: La Marmotte is described as one of the hardest grand fondos in the world, featuring four huge climbs totaling 65 kilometers of climbing and 5,000 meters of vertical gain. The course is significantly longer than a typical Tour de France mountain stage, with most riders taking 8-12 hours to finish and many abandoning. Key challenges include dramatic elevation changes, with sections like the balcony road from Alpe d'Huez offering scenic but scary descents of 800 meters. Riders emphasize two critical success factors: proper pacing on the climbs and consistent fueling throughout the long day. The race starts early from Bourg d'Oisans, and riders report concerns about hot weather conditions affecting performance. Preparation involves careful tapering with 50% volume reduction in final days and strategic carb loading on Thursday-Friday before the event.
